Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle (26) - What That #! Line at the Top Actually Means
Let me give my brain a break this chapter. We've learned a lot of stuff already, so let's keep it simple this time.
Have you ever noticed that a lot of Python files have this strange line at the very top? Something like:
So what do these lines actually mean?
In short, they just tell the system which Python interpreter to use.

At this point you might be wondering why you'd ever need to specify the Python interpreter. The reason is that some people have both Python 2 and Python 3 installed on the same machine. Me, for example.

And Python 2 and Python 3 are incompatible, both in terms of packages and syntax. In earlier chapters I've thrown in a few examples of where Python 2 and Python 3 differ, so you've probably noticed the syntax isn't always the same.

). Its job is to specify the type of interpreter — well, "type" is the textbook explanation, but really it's pointing at the path to the interpreter's executable file. So it's not just a Python thing. You'll see it in PHP too:

This line is mainly used on "Unix-like" systems. Unix-like just means the various versions that branched off from Unix, but that's a rabbit hole I don't want to go down here. If you want to dig into what "Unix-like" means, I'd recommend reading the Wikipedia explanation

So how do we actually verify that this line works? If you're a Mac developer, congrats — you can try it out yourself. But if you're a Windows developer, all I can say is sorry, because Windows doesn't support shebang.

So based on that meme, you already know the rest of this chapter won't involve Windows at all. We're sticking to Mac. So why bother specifying the interpreter? Back in the install chapter we explained that Mac ships with Python 2.x built in, and if you want Python 3.x you usually have to install it separately.
See where this is going? A single Mac ends up with two versions of Python on it. We haven't run into many problems so far, because we've been explicitly using the
command to run our Python files. To put it in Node.js terms... it's kind of like how you usually type
to run Node.js, and then imagine one day Node.js does a big version bump and from then on you'd have to type
So you might be thinking, "Do I still need to write

then?" My answer is yeah, it's still a good idea. Besides making sure your Python file runs fine on a dual-version (Python 2 and Python 3) Unix system, that one line also tells you at a glance whether the file is meant to run on Python 2 or Python 3.

Oh, right. Besides specifying the interpreter with

, there's also a way to specify the source file's character encoding. Python usually defaults to UTF-8 (also known as Unicode), but there are always edge cases. So when you want to change the encoding, you just write the encoding you want at the top of the file:
That said, sticking with UTF-8 is generally the better call. So you'll also see people hard-code it just to dodge any weirdness:
At this point you might be wondering why I've never written this interpreter-specifying line in any of my files. The reason is simple: I forgot.
Alright, that's about time. This was just a quick and easy chat about shebang. See you in the next one.
